narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model designed to forecast first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students. The model assessed your profile and predicted a 47.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, tipping the scales just enough to result in a rejection of your admission. This threshold reflects the school's cautious approach to admitting students likely to pass the bar and succeed in practice. The three most influential factors in this decision were your LSAT score, your Year 3 school decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA, each pulling the prediction in different directions with nearly equal strength. Your LSAT score of 32.50, notably below the average of 36.70, was the top positive influencer, nudging the model toward predicting failure because lower scores like yours have historically correlated with bar struggles among similar students. On the flip side, your Year 3 decile of 2—well below the typical 5.53 average—was the strongest driver toward failure, signaling to the model that your performance in that key later year placed you in the lower ranks compared to peers who tend to pass. Balancing these, your above-average undergrad GPA pushed strongly in the favorable direction, suggesting solid foundational skills that could support bar success. Overall, your full-time student status aligned with the vast majority (over 92%) of successful applicants, and your middle family income bracket was right around average, so they played lesser roles here. Compared to typical students who get approved, your combination stood out for that low Year 3 decile dragging against your strengths elsewhere, creating uncertainty the model couldn't overlook. While this is disappointing, especially with your respectable GPA, it's not a definitive verdict on your potential—models like this capture patterns, not destinies. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and reapplying with those updates to shift the prediction in your favor. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these exact areas, and with your profile's strengths, you're well-positioned to improve your odds next time.
